locked_facts: Parallel: C_total = C₁ + C₂ + C₃ + ... (capacitances add directly)
In parallel, voltage across each capacitor is the same
In series, charge on each capacitor is the same
Energy stored in a capacitor: U = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/2C
Series combination always gives a total capacitance less than the smallest individual capacitor
Parallel combination always gives a total capacitance greater than the largest individual capacitor
truth_statements: When capacitors are connected in parallel the potential difference across each is the same and the total capacitance is the sum of individual capacitances
When capacitors are connected in series the charge on each capacitor is the same and the reciprocal of total capacitance equals the sum of reciprocals
The energy stored in a capacitor is ½CV²
Adding a capacitor in parallel always increases total capacitance while adding one in series always decreases it
For two capacitors in series the total capacitance is C₁C₂/(C₁+C₂)

aha_moment.what_to_show: Same two capacitors shown first in parallel (C_total = C₁+C₂ = large) then in series (C_total = C₁C₂/(C₁+C₂) = small). Charge bars and voltage bars animate to show WHY: parallel shares voltage, series shares charge.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Side-by-side parallel vs series with animated charge flow and voltage labels
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students confuse series/parallel rules for capacitors with resistors — seeing charge conservation in series vs voltage equality in parallel makes the logic clear

simulation_states.label: Single capacitor
simulation_states.description: One capacitor connected to a battery. Charge Q accumulates on plates, voltage V equals battery voltage. Energy bar shows U = ½CV².
simulation_states.emphasis: Q = CV is the fundamental relationship

simulation_states.label: Parallel combination
simulation_states.description: Two capacitors in parallel across the same battery. Both have same voltage V. Total charge = Q₁ + Q₂. C_total = C₁ + C₂ shown numerically.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same V across both — charges ADD — so capacitances ADD

simulation_states.label: Series combination
simulation_states.description: Same two capacitors now in series. Charge Q is identical on both. Voltages V₁ and V₂ add to battery voltage. 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same Q on both — voltages ADD — so reciprocals of C add

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student toggles between series and parallel, adjusts C₁ and C₂. Charge and voltage distributions update in real time. Energy comparison bar shows which stores more energy.
simulation_states.emphasis: Switch between series and parallel — watch C_total, Q, and V change

common_misconceptions.belief: Capacitors in series add directly like resistors in series
common_misconceptions.correction: Capacitors in series follow the reciprocal rule: 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂. This is OPPOSITE to resistors. In series, charge is the same on each capacitor, and voltages add up.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show same charge Q on both series capacitors with voltages adding

common_misconceptions.belief: Capacitors in parallel have the same charge
common_misconceptions.correction: In parallel, voltage is the same across each capacitor but charge distributes: Q₁ = C₁V and Q₂ = C₂V — larger capacitor stores more charge
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show different charge amounts on parallel capacitors with same voltage
